steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim states Newland ‘compares his intellect favorably’ to other men. A supporting quotation must show him directly measuring his mind against theirs and coming out ahead.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'felt distinctly the superior… had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world': An explicit intellectual comparison with Newland on top. ✓
  • Choice B — 'thinking over a pleasure… gave him subtler satisfaction': Describes his taste for anticipation, not an intellectual comparison with others. ✗
  • Choice C — 'no expense had been spared on the setting… acknowledged beautiful': Describes the opera house; no comparison of minds at all. ✗
  • Choice D — 'singly they betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented New York': Touches on social conformity, not on reading, thinking, or intellectual standing. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
